retrieved.title: Minimum number of credits
heading_path: For your information
retrieved.text:
If you study full-time, you are expected to obtain 120 ECTS credits within four semesters according to the curriculum. The majority of students achieves 16 ECTS credits within one semester, for example by completing the introductory and orientation period (STEOP).

There is no mandatory study pace apart from the minimum number of credits. You decide yourself how fast you study and how many ECTS credits you complete per semester. However, please note that financial aids and scholarships may be subject to stricter rules.

The minimum number of credits does not apply to

- students with proven physical/mental impairments
- bachelor’s or diploma programmes to which you have been admitted before the winter semester of 2022/23
- master’s and doctoral programmes

Information about the [recognition of the additional exam Latin](https://klassischephilologie.univie.ac.at/studium/ergaenzungspruefungen/latein-ergaenzungspruefung/) for the minimum number of credits can be found (in German only) on the website of the Institute for Classical Philology, Medieval and Modern Latin.

retrieved.title: Minimum number of credits
retrieved.text:
You have to obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in every bachelor’s and diploma programme within four semesters.

This applies

- to all programmes to which you are admitted since the winter semester of 2022/2023
- and to all programmes that you [resume](/en/admission/resumption-of-studies/) from this time.

Otherwise, the admission to the degree programme expires. If you would like to apply for re-admission to the same degree programme (= resumption of studies) at the University of Vienna, you can only do so after two [academic years](/en/semester-planning/the-academic-year/) (= four semesters).

retrieved.title: ABC of terminology
heading_path: E
retrieved.text: / - ECTS credits The "European Credit Transfer System (ECTS)" contributes to the mobility between degree programmes, universities and countries, as ECTS credits facilitate a comparison of academic achievements. ECTS credits describe the average workload required to achieve the objectives of a course, module or degree programme. One ECTS credit corresponds to an average workload of 25 full hours. Course participation as well as the preparation and follow-up of a course, the duration of the exam and studying time are considered as part of the workload. In u:find you can see how many ECTS credits you have to obtain to successfully complete a course. Example: Max attends a seminar comprising 4 ECTS credits (= 100 hours). The seminar takes place ten times and takes 1.5 hours each time (= 15 hours). He has to prepare a presentation and a summary (which takes him 30 hours), a seminar paper (30 hours) and he has to read texts for every second session (5 hours each). Over the course of the semester, he will be working 100 hours for the seminar. retrieved.title: Extension curricula and alternative extensions
retrieved.text:
Almost all bachelor’s programmes require students to complete extension curricula (EC) in addition to the regular compulsory and elective subjects. An EC is a predifined module group of up to 30 ECTS credits. The required number of ECTS credits is defined in the curriculum of your degree programme.

In order to complete an EC, you must first register for the EC in [u:space](/en/web-services-uspace-ufind-moodle-webmail/#c216328). You will be able to register for individual courses of the EC after having passed the STEOP.

Nearly all degree programmes offer extension curricula. Unless otherwise specified, you may freely choose from the range of EC offered.

You can also complete [alternative extensions (AE)](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c273245) of a number of at least 15 ECTS instead of an EC in most bachelor's degree programmes..

- If you have questions about the content of an EC and the recognition of examination results from an EC, please get in touch with the [studies service unit (SSS)/studies service center (SSC)](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for the EC.
- If you have questions about the recognition of an EC for your degree programme, please contact the [SSS/SSC](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for your degree programme.

- [Alternative extensions (AE)](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c228260)

retrieved.text: exams. # FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity) / - When are students ‘prüfungsaktiv’? Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? retrieved.title: Exemptions from the entrance exam procedure
heading_path: 60 ECTS exemption for the diploma programme in Law
retrieved.text:
You are exempt from the entrance exam procedure for the diploma programme in Law if you

- were admitted to a jurisprudential degree programme at a recognised educational institution and
- if you can prove having achieved at least 60 ECTS.

The 60 ECTS have to be recognisable for compulsory or elective subjects or compulsory modules of the diploma programme in Law at the University of Vienna. The StudiesServiceCenter Law has created an [overview about recognitions](https://ssc-rechtswissenschaften.univie.ac.at/diplomstudium-neu-2025/anerkennung/) (only available in German). Please check beforehand if your achievements can be recognised.

You do not have to obtain the minimum number of credits for each teaching subject separately. If one teaching subject in your combination changes, as long as one of the two teaching subjects remains the same, the semesters continue to be counted for the new combination. Both newly obtained ECTS credits and ECTS credits recognised in due time are counting towards the ECTS credits for the new combination. If you already obtained the minimum number of credits for the old combination, you do not have to obtain them for the new one again. reranked.text: / - ECTS credits The "European Credit Transfer System (ECTS)" contributes to the mobility between degree programmes, universities and countries, as ECTS credits facilitate a comparison of academic achievements. ECTS credits describe the average workload required to achieve the objectives of a course, module or degree programme. One ECTS credit corresponds to an average workload of 25 full hours. Course participation as well as the preparation and follow-up of a course, the duration of the exam and studying time are considered as part of the workload. In u:find you can see how many ECTS credits you have to obtain to successfully complete a course. Example: Max attends a seminar comprising 4 ECTS credits (= 100 hours). The seminar takes place ten times and takes 1.5 hours each time (= 15 hours). He has to prepare a presentation and a summary (which takes him 30 hours), a seminar paper (30 hours) and he has to read texts for every second session (5 hours each). Over the course of the semester, he will be working 100 hours for the seminar. reranked.title: Extension curricula and alternative extensions
reranked.text:
Almost all bachelor’s programmes require students to complete extension curricula (EC) in addition to the regular compulsory and elective subjects. An EC is a predifined module group of up to 30 ECTS credits. The required number of ECTS credits is defined in the curriculum of your degree programme.

In order to complete an EC, you must first register for the EC in [u:space](/en/web-services-uspace-ufind-moodle-webmail/#c216328). You will be able to register for individual courses of the EC after having passed the STEOP.

Nearly all degree programmes offer extension curricula. Unless otherwise specified, you may freely choose from the range of EC offered.

You can also complete [alternative extensions (AE)](/en/abc-of-terminology/?no_cache=1#c273245) of a number of at least 15 ECTS instead of an EC in most bachelor's degree programmes..

- If you have questions about the content of an EC and the recognition of examination results from an EC, please get in touch with the [studies service unit (SSS)/studies service center (SSC)](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for the EC.
- If you have questions about the recognition of an EC for your degree programme, please contact the [SSS/SSC](http://www.univie.ac.at/en/about-us/governance-structure/organisation-of-studies-courses/) responsible for your degree programme.

reranked.text: exams. # FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity) / - When are students ‘prüfungsaktiv’? Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? reranked.title: Exemptions from the entrance exam procedure
heading_path: 60 ECTS exemption for the diploma programme in Law
reranked.text:
You are exempt from the entrance exam procedure for the diploma programme in Law if you

- were admitted to a jurisprudential degree programme at a recognised educational institution and
- if you can prove having achieved at least 60 ECTS.

The 60 ECTS have to be recognisable for compulsory or elective subjects or compulsory modules of the diploma programme in Law at the University of Vienna. The StudiesServiceCenter Law has created an [overview about recognitions](https://ssc-rechtswissenschaften.univie.ac.at/diplomstudium-neu-2025/anerkennung/) (only available in German). Please check beforehand if your achievements can be recognised.

selected.title: Minimum number of credits
heading_path: For your information
selected.text:
If you study full-time, you are expected to obtain 120 ECTS credits within four semesters according to the curriculum. The majority of students achieves 16 ECTS credits within one semester, for example by completing the introductory and orientation period (STEOP).

There is no mandatory study pace apart from the minimum number of credits. You decide yourself how fast you study and how many ECTS credits you complete per semester. However, please note that financial aids and scholarships may be subject to stricter rules.

The minimum number of credits does not apply to

- students with proven physical/mental impairments
- bachelor’s or diploma programmes to which you have been admitted before the winter semester of 2022/23
- master’s and doctoral programmes

Information about the [recognition of the additional exam Latin](https://klassischephilologie.univie.ac.at/studium/ergaenzungspruefungen/latein-ergaenzungspruefung/) for the minimum number of credits can be found (in German only) on the website of the Institute for Classical Philology, Medieval and Modern Latin.

selected.title: Minimum number of credits
selected.text:
You have to obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in every bachelor’s and diploma programme within four semesters.

This applies

- to all programmes to which you are admitted since the winter semester of 2022/2023
- and to all programmes that you [resume](/en/admission/resumption-of-studies/) from this time.

Otherwise, the admission to the degree programme expires. If you would like to apply for re-admission to the same degree programme (= resumption of studies) at the University of Vienna, you can only do so after two [academic years](/en/semester-planning/the-academic-year/) (= four semesters).
